explicit_text: I was born and raised in Mogadishu, the capital city of Somalia. It is where I attended primary school and later graduated from Wadajir Secondary School in 1984. Mogadishu was my home until the Somali Civil War forced me to leave in 1991. The city holds many memories of my early life and education.

explicit_text: Somalia is my homeland where I was born and spent my early years. After finishing secondary school in 1984, I completed a year of national service there, teaching Arabic at an adult literacy school. I started my own business with government support in Somalia before the central government was overthrown in 1991. The civil war that followed forced me to leave Somalia and seek refuge elsewhere, but I always carry my national ID as a symbol of the Somali government and my roots.

explicit_text: The Somali Civil War began when the central government was overthrown by rebels in 1991. This conflict disrupted my business and life in Somalia, forcing me to leave the country for safety. The war was a turning point that led me to seek asylum in Uganda and later resettlement in the United States. It marked the loss of the Somali government and the stability I once knew.

explicit_text: After fleeing Somalia due to the civil war in 1991, I sought asylum in Uganda. While there, my Somali national ID was very important for proving my identity among many refugees. Uganda was a temporary home where I lived until the United States offered me resettlement under their refugee program. My time in Uganda was marked by survival and hope for a new beginning.

explicit_text: In 2005, I was given the opportunity to resettle in the United States through a refugee program. Moving to the U.S. was a new chapter in my life after years in Uganda. I brought my Somali national ID with me as a treasured reminder of my homeland and its lost government. The U.S. provided me with safety and a chance to rebuild my life.

explicit_text: When I arrived in the United States in 2005, I settled in Minnesota and have lived here ever since. Minnesota became my new home where I continued to cherish my Somali identity and memories. The community here has been part of my journey as a refugee and immigrant, and I hold onto my national ID as a symbol of my heritage to pass on to my children.
